title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
text:
Mathematical errors are commonly explained as failures of calculation, memory, or logical reasoning. A learner applies the wrong operation, forgets a rule, or makes an invalid deduction, and the visible mistake is treated as the point at which understanding failed. Yet some errors begin earlier. The learner may perform each local operation correctly while silently changing what the symbols are understood to represent. The notation remains unchanged, but the conceptual situation in which the notation is being interpreted has shifted.
A mathematical expression is not a self-contained object whose full meaning can always be recovered from its visible symbols alone. Symbols constrain interpretation, but their usable meaning can only be acquired through definitions, conventions, surrounding language, diagrams, and the mathematical situation under consideration. The expression \(5/5\), for example, can be interpreted through a part–whole model, in which one whole has been partitioned into five equal parts and all five parts are selected. It can also be interpreted as a quotient, in which five units are divided or distributed into five equal groups. Both interpretations lead to the numerical value \(1\), but they organize the situation differently.
This difference matters because mathematical reasoning does not operate on symbols alone. It operates on symbols as interpreted within a conceptual model. In the part–whole model, the denominator \(5\) describes the number of equal parts into which one whole has been partitioned, while the numerator \(5\) describes how many of those parts are being considered. The numerator does not mean that five independent wholes existed before the partitioning occurred. If the learner suddenly begins imagining five original objects being divided among five groups, the expression has been transferred into a quotient model. That second interpretation is legitimate, but it is not the same situation as the first.






flowchart TD
    A["Visible expression<br/>5/5"] --> B["Part–whole model"]
    A --> C["Quotient model"]
    B --> D["One whole partitioned into 5 equal parts<br/>5 parts selected"]
    C --> E["5 units divided into 5 equal groups"]
    D --> F["Numerical value: 1"]
    E --> F




Figure 1: The visible expression remains the same, but different conceptual models assign different roles to its components.





The diagram reveals an important distinction. In both cases, \(5/5=1\), but the objects, roles, and relationships represented by the two occurrences of \(5\) are not identical. A learner who moves deliberately between these interpretations can use their connection to deepen understanding. A learner who moves between them without recognizing the transition may combine assumptions from one model with deductions belonging to another. We will call this unnoticed transition silent contextual switching. The term refers to a change in the conceptual model used to interpret an expression when the change is neither recognized nor explicitly reconstructed.






flowchart LR
    A["Expression in Model A"] --> B["Reasoning begins"]
    B --> C{"Model changes?"}
    C -->|"No"| D["Continue within established meanings"]
    C -->|"Yes, explicitly"| E["Reassign objects, roles, and relationships"]
    C -->|"Yes, unnoticed"| F["Old assumptions enter a new model"]
    E --> G["Context-preserving reasoning"]
    F --> H["Conceptual confusion"]




Figure 2: A contextual transition becomes dangerous when the expression is carried into a new model without reassigning the meanings of its components.





Morever, the contextual change loses the preservation of semantic control during that change. Before reasoning from an expression, the learner must know what kind of objects are being represented, what role each symbol plays, which relationships are currently assumed, and which conclusions are valid within that interpretation. When another interpretation becomes useful, the learner must identify what has changed and what has remained invariant. The symbols may be preserved, and the numerical value may be preserved, while the meanings assigned to the symbols are reorganized.
This article investigates how such contextual control can become part of mathematical reasoning. It asks why visible features of notation can trigger unintended interpretations, why beginners are especially vulnerable to unnoticed model changes, how valid mathematical meanings become confused when they are combined without translation, and how explanations can help learners preserve conceptual coherence. Its purpose is not to teach a single meaning of fractions or to discourage movement between mathematical representations. Its purpose is to develop a framework through which learners can move between meanings deliberately.
The guiding question of the article is therefore:

How can a learner preserve the meaning of a mathematical expression while reasoning within one conceptual model and moving deliberately into another?

The next section examines \(5/5\) more closely, not as an arithmetic exercise, but as a case in which one visible expression can belong to two valid mathematical worlds.

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
text:
2.1 The Part–Whole Model
In the part–whole model, reasoning begins with one whole. The whole is partitioned into five equal parts, and all five parts are selected. The denominator tells us how many equal parts constitute the chosen partition of the whole, while the numerator tells us how many parts of that size are being considered.
\[
\frac{5}{5}
=
\frac{\text{five selected parts}}
     {\text{five equal parts in the whole}}
=
1\text{ whole}.
\]
The numerator \(5\) does not represent five original wholes. It represents five parts, each having the size \(1/5\) of the original whole. Their combined quantity is therefore
\[
5\times\frac{1}{5}=1.
\]
The reasoning can be represented structurally:






flowchart LR
    A["One whole"] --> B["Partition into 5 equal parts"]
    B --> C["Each part has size 1/5"]
    C --> D["Select all 5 parts"]
    D --> E["5 × 1/5 = 1 whole"]




Figure 3: In the part–whole model, five refers to the number of selected fifth-sized parts, not to five original wholes.





The important object at the beginning of this model is one whole. The number \(5\) enters only after a partition has been chosen. It describes how the whole has been organized and how many resulting parts are under consideration. If the learner begins imagining five independent objects before the partition takes place, the learner is no longer reasoning within the original part–whole model.

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
text:
2.2 The Quotient Model
The same expression can also be interpreted as a division problem. Suppose five units are distributed equally among five recipients. The amount received by each recipient is
\[
5\div5=1.
\]
Here, the first \(5\) represents the total quantity being distributed, while the second \(5\) represents the number of equal recipient groups. The result \(1\) represents the amount belonging to each group.






flowchart LR
    A["Total quantity<br/>5 units"] --> B["Distribute equally"]
    C["Number of groups<br/>5"] --> B
    B --> D["Quantity per group<br/>1 unit"]




Figure 4: In the quotient model, the two occurrences of five represent a total quantity and a number of groups.





This interpretation is mathematically legitimate. A fraction bar represents division, and every fraction \(a/b\), with \(b\neq0\), can be understood as the quotient \(a\div b\). The problem does not arise because the learner recognizes this connection. The problem arises when the learner enters the quotient model without noticing that the roles of the numbers have been reassigned.

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
text:
2.3 What Changes Between the Models
The two models can be compared directly:








Feature
Part–whole interpretation
Quotient interpretation




Starting situation
One whole
Five units


First \(5\)
Number of selected fifth-sized parts
Total quantity being distributed


Second \(5\)
Number of equal parts constituting the whole
Number of equal groups


Result
All parts reconstruct one whole
Each group receives one unit


Preserved value
\(1\)
\(1\)



This allows us to locate the learner’s confusion precisely. The thought “\(5/5\) means five things divided by five things” is not inherently false. It is a possible quotient interpretation. The confusion begins when the learner treats the first \(5\) in that statement as though it were still referring to the five selected parts from the part–whole model while simultaneously treating those parts as five independent original units. The mind has retained the symbol \(5\) but changed what the symbol counts.






flowchart TD
    A["Symbol: numerator 5"] --> B["Part–whole meaning<br/>5 selected fifth-sized parts"]
    A --> C["Quotient meaning<br/>5 units available for division"]
    B --> D["Together equal one original whole"]
    C --> E["Exist as the total quantity before distribution"]
    B -. "unnoticed replacement" .-> C




Figure 5: Silent contextual switching preserves the visible symbol while replacing the object that the symbol represents.





The conceptual error therefore occurs at the level of reference rather than arithmetic. The learner has not necessarily calculated incorrectly. Instead, the learner has allowed a number to begin referring to a different kind of object without reconstructing the surrounding model. Once this substitution occurs, later predictions and deductions may become unreliable because they are built from assumptions that originated in different mathematical situations.
A deliberate transition between the two models would make the change explicit: “Five fifth-sized parts reconstruct one whole. The numerical fraction \(5/5\) is also equal to the quotient \(5\div5\). In that quotient interpretation, however, the first \(5\) now represents a total quantity rather than five parts previously created from one whole.” This statement preserves the mathematical connection while marking the change in meaning.
The lesson is not that a learner must choose one permanent interpretation of \(5/5\). The requirement is that each interpretation remain internally coherent and that movement between them include an explicit reassignment of objects, roles, and relationships. The next section examines why the mind often makes such transitions silently, especially when the visible notation strongly suggests another familiar operation.

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
text:
3.1 Familiar Interpretations Compete for Control
A learner often possesses several partially developed meanings for the same expression. One meaning may have been learned through shaded shapes, another through sharing objects, and another through the procedural statement that “a fraction means division.” When an expression is encountered, these interpretations do not necessarily appear in an orderly sequence. A familiar or recently practised interpretation may become active before the learner checks whether it matches the current situation.
This explains why a learner may begin correctly with one whole divided into five equal parts and then suddenly imagine five objects being divided into five groups. The second image may be easier to retrieve because division has repeatedly been taught through sharing. The mind uses the most available pattern to complete the meaning of the notation. Prior knowledge therefore performs two opposing functions. Well-organized knowledge helps a learner recognize valid connections between models. Incomplete or weakly connected knowledge can cause one familiar model to intrude upon another.

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
section: 3.2 Numerical Agreement Can Conceal Conceptual Change
text:
3.2 Numerical Agreement Can Conceal Conceptual Change
Silent switching is especially difficult to detect when both models produce the same numerical result. In the part–whole model,
\[
5\times\frac{1}{5}=1,
\]
while in the quotient model,
\[
5\div5=1.
\]
Because both paths end at \(1\), the final answer provides no evidence that the learner changed the meaning of the expression during reasoning. A correct answer can therefore conceal an unstable conceptual process.






flowchart LR
    A["Part–whole path<br/>5 fifths reconstruct a whole"] --> C["Answer: 1"]
    B["Quotient path<br/>5 units divided into 5 groups"] --> C
    C --> D["Numerical agreement"]
    D --> E["Contextual change may remain undetected"]




Figure 7: Agreement in the final numerical value can hide a change in the conceptual path used to reach it.





When two models happen to agree, the learner may conclude that their objects and relationships are interchangeable. That assumption can then be carried into a problem where the distinction affects what can validly be inferred. Correct calculation in the present problem does not guarantee conceptual stability for future reasoning.

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
section: 3.3 Procedural Language Can Suppress the Model
text:
3.3 Procedural Language Can Suppress the Model
Mathematical instruction often compresses meaning into short rules: “the numerator is on top,” “the denominator is on the bottom,” “a fraction is division,” or “divide both sides.” Such statements can support fluency, but they identify visible positions and procedures more readily than conceptual roles. A learner may remember where a number appears or which operation to perform without preserving what the number represents.
For example, saying only that \(5/5\) means \(5\div5\) is formally correct, but it can prematurely replace the part–whole interpretation that the learner was constructing. The explanation supplies an operational equivalence without explaining the translation between models. The learner sees that the same symbols can be calculated through division but may not see how five selected fifth-sized parts correspond to the quotient representation.
A well-designed explanation should therefore distinguish between three claims:







Claim
What it establishes




\(\frac{5}{5}\) represents five fifth-sized parts
Meaning within the part–whole model


\(\frac{5}{5}=5\div5\)
Formal connection between fraction and quotient


Both expressions have the value \(1\)
Numerical invariant preserved across the models

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
section: 3.4 Attention Usually Follows What Changes Visibly
text:
3.4 Attention Usually Follows What Changes Visibly
Human attention is naturally drawn to visible changes. If an equation gains a new term, if a diagram changes shape, or if an operation sign is replaced, the learner has an external cue that something new must be interpreted. Silent contextual switching is harder to detect because the crucial change is not visible. The expression remains \(5/5\); only the internally assigned referents change.
This produces an asymmetry between notation and thought:






flowchart LR
    A["External notation<br/>5/5"] --> B["No visible change"]
    C["Assigned meaning<br/>selected parts"] --> D["Assigned meaning<br/>divisible units"]
    B --> E["Attention is not alerted"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Context switch remains silent"]




Figure 8: During silent contextual switching, the external representation remains stable while the internally assigned meanings change.





The learner must therefore monitor something that the notation does not display directly: the continuing relationship between each symbol and the object it represents. This is a form of semantic monitoring. It asks not only, “What operation am I performing?” but also, “What does each quantity refer to now, and is that the same thing it referred to one step earlier?”The next section examines the consequences of this unnoticed transition. It shows how assumptions from one model can enter another, producing deductions that appear locally reasonable but are globally incoherent.

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
section: 4.1 A Number Does Not Preserve Its Referent Automatically
text:
4.1 A Number Does Not Preserve Its Referent Automatically
A numeral tells us how many, but it does not independently tell us how many of what. The first \(5\) in the part–whole interpretation counts fifth-sized parts:
\[
5\text{ parts}\times
\frac{1\text{ whole}}{5\text{ parts}}
=
1\text{ whole}.
\]
The units written into this relationship expose what the bare notation can conceal. Five fifth-sized parts do not constitute five wholes. The numerical count is \(5\), but the counted objects are parts whose individual size is \(1/5\) of a whole.
In the quotient interpretation, the first \(5\) may instead describe a total of five units:
\[
\frac{5\text{ units}}{5\text{ groups}}
=
1\text{ unit per group}.
\]
Removing the words parts, whole, units, and groups compresses both relationships into the same visible expression. This compression is mathematically useful, but it places responsibility on the learner to preserve information that the notation no longer displays explicitly.






flowchart TD
    A["Visible numeral<br/>5"] --> B["Counts selected parts"]
    A --> C["Counts distributable units"]
    B --> D["Each object has size 1/5 of a whole"]
    C --> E["Each object is treated as one unit"]
    D --> F["5 parts reconstruct 1 whole"]
    E --> G["5 units form the quantity being divided"]




Figure 9: The numeral five remains visible while the kind of object being counted changes.





The numeral is therefore not the complete meaning. A more complete semantic unit consists of the number together with its referent, role, and relationships:
\[
\text{mathematical quantity}
=
\text{numerical value}
+
\text{kind of object}
+
\text{role in the model}.
\]

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
text:
4.2 Four Forms of Contextual Drift
When a learner silently changes models, several kinds of meaning can drift:








Form of drift
What changes unnoticed
Example




Referent drift
The object to which a number or symbol refers
Five selected parts become five original units


Role drift
The mathematical function of an element
The numerator changes from a count of parts to a dividend


Relational drift
The relationship connecting the quantities
Partitioning one whole becomes distributing several units


Temporal drift
What exists before and after an operation
Parts created by partitioning are treated as objects that existed beforehand



These forms of drift often occur together. In the \(5/5\) example, the referent changes from parts to independent units, the numerator becomes a dividend, partitioning becomes distribution, and the five objects are moved from after the partition to before the division. The learner may experience this as one small change in imagery, although it has reorganized the entire conceptual situation.

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
section: 4.3 The Question Can Change Along With the Model
text:
4.3 The Question Can Change Along With the Model
Contextual switching can also change the question being answered. In the part–whole model, the relevant question is:

How much of the original whole is represented by five of its five equal parts?

In the quotient model, the question becomes:

How much does each group receive when five units are divided equally among five groups?

Both questions have the answer \(1\), but the answer describes something different in each case. In the first, it describes the proportion of the original whole that has been selected. In the second, it describes the quantity assigned to each group. This reveals why checking only the numerical answer is insufficient. A complete check must ask:
\[
\text{What does the answer represent in the current model?}
\]

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
section: 4.4 From Procedural Consistency to Semantic Consistency
text:
4.4 From Procedural Consistency to Semantic Consistency
Semantic consistency requires continuity at three levels:






flowchart TD
    A["Referents<br/>What objects do the symbols represent?"] --> D["Semantically coherent reasoning"]
    B["Roles<br/>What function does each quantity perform?"] --> D
    C["Relationships<br/>How are the quantities connected?"] --> D




Figure 10: Coherent reasoning preserves or explicitly reassigns referents, roles, and relationships.





Mathematical coherence therefore requires either preservation or explicit translation: meanings must remain stable within a model, or they must be deliberately reassigned when moving into another model. The next section develops a practical framework for achieving this control.

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
text:
5.1 Identify the Active Model
Before manipulating an expression, the learner should establish what mathematical situation is currently being represented. This requires more than naming a topic such as “fractions” or “division.” A topic can contain several conceptual models. The learner must identify the objects involved, the operation or relationship connecting them, and the question being answered.
For \(5/5\), saying only that the expression is “a fraction” does not identify the model. A more complete statement would be:

One whole has been partitioned into five equal parts, and five of those parts are being considered.

This statement identifies the starting object, the partitioning action, the resulting parts, and the selection being represented. It establishes the conceptual environment within which the numerator and denominator will receive their meanings.
A useful orienting question is:

What situation must be true for this expression to represent what I currently claim it represents?

This question forces the learner to reconstruct the background that compact notation may have hidden.

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
section: 5.2 Bind Each Symbol to a Referent and Role
text:
5.2 Bind Each Symbol to a Referent and Role
After identifying the model, the learner must determine what each important symbol refers to and what function it performs. A referent is the object or quantity represented by a symbol. A role is the function that the represented quantity performs within the current relationship.
In the part–whole model of \(5/5\), the bindings are:








Symbol
Referent
Role




Denominator \(5\)
The five equal parts constituting the chosen partition
Determines the size of each part as \(1/5\) of the whole


Numerator \(5\)
The five selected parts
Counts how many fifth-sized parts are considered


Fraction bar
Relationship between selected parts and the partition
Forms the fraction and connects it formally to division


Value \(1\)
The quantity represented by all five fifths
Expresses one reconstructed whole

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
text:
5.3 Preserve Meanings During Deduction
Once meanings have been assigned, they should remain stable while reasoning continues within the same model. Every new statement should use the symbols in a way compatible with their established referents and roles.
Starting from the part–whole model, the following deduction preserves meaning:
\[
\frac{5}{5}
=
5\times\frac{1}{5}
=
1.
\]
The first \(5\) continues to count selected parts, and \(1/5\) continues to describe the size of each part relative to the original whole. The conclusion \(1\) therefore means that the selected parts reconstruct one whole.
Semantic preservation can be checked by restoring the hidden nouns:
\[
5\text{ selected parts}
\times
\frac{1\text{ whole}}{5\text{ parts}}
=
1\text{ whole}.
\]
Restoring units or referents is useful because symbolic compression can hide a change that becomes obvious when the represented objects are named.

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
text:
5.4 Detect a Possible Contextual Transition
A learner should pause when an expression activates another familiar interpretation. Common transition cues include changing from a diagram to an equation, replacing a fraction bar with a division symbol, introducing a new word for an existing quantity, applying a memorized rule, or interpreting a symbol through a recently learned procedure.
The relevant question is not merely:

Is this new interpretation mathematically valid?

It is also:

Am I still talking about the same objects in the same relationships?

For example, rewriting
\[
\frac{5}{5}=5\div5
\]
is formally valid. Nevertheless, the explicit division notation may activate a sharing model. That activation marks a possible contextual boundary. Before continuing, the learner should determine whether the quotient is being used merely as an equivalent operation or whether an entirely new situation is being imagined.






flowchart LR
    A["Current meaning<br/>5 selected fifths"] --> B["New representation<br/>5 ÷ 5"]
    B --> C{"Same model or new model?"}
    C -->|"Equivalent operation"| D["Retain established referents"]
    C -->|"New sharing situation"| E["Reassign referents explicitly"]




Figure 12: A transition cue should trigger a semantic check before reasoning continues.

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
text:
5.5 Translate Rather Than Merely Replace
When a new model is useful, the learner should translate into it. Translation differs from silent replacement because it states how the elements of the original model correspond to those of the new one and where that correspondence stops.
A deliberate transition might be expressed as follows:

In the part–whole model, \(5/5\) represents five parts, each having size \(1/5\) of one whole. Numerically, this fraction is equivalent to \(5\div5\). If we now interpret the expression through a sharing model, the first \(5\) will represent a total quantity of five units and the second \(5\) will represent five groups. The value remains \(1\), but the objects and the meaning of the result have changed.

This translation makes three facts visible: the new model has begun, the two occurrences of \(5\) have acquired new referents, and only certain properties—such as the numerical value—are preserved.

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
text:
5.6 Verify Changes and Invariants
The final action is to determine what changed during the transition and what remained invariant. An invariant is a property preserved across representations or models. In the transition between the part–whole and quotient interpretations of \(5/5\), the symbolic expression and numerical value remain invariant. The starting objects, roles of the two fives, represented operation, and interpretation of the answer change.



Feature
Preserved or changed?




Written expression \(5/5\)
Preserved


Numerical value \(1\)
Preserved


Starting quantity
Changed


Meaning of the first \(5\)
Changed


Meaning of the second \(5\)
Changed


Relationship between the quantities
Changed


Meaning of the result
Changed

title: When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
text:
5.7 Applying the Complete Framework
The entire process can be applied to \(5/5\) as a coherent sequence:

Identify the model: one whole is partitioned into five equal parts, and all five are selected.
Bind the symbols: the denominator counts equal parts in the partition; the numerator counts selected fifth-sized parts.
Preserve meaning: reason that five copies of \(1/5\) reconstruct one whole.
Detect the transition: notice that reading the fraction bar as division may activate a quotient model.
Translate explicitly: state that the first \(5\) now represents a total quantity and the second represents a number of groups.
Verify invariants: recognize that the value \(1\) is preserved while the conceptual roles change.

This framework adds semantic supervision to mathematical reasoning. Ordinary procedural supervision asks whether each operation is permitted. Semantic supervision asks whether each operation is still being performed on the objects that the symbols were established to represent. Reliable reasoning requires both.
The framework is not limited to fractions. Similar contextual transitions occur when a negative number changes from representing a position to representing a displacement, when a letter changes from an unknown constant to a varying quantity, when equality changes from announcing an answer to expressing a relation, or when a probability changes from a long-run frequency interpretation to a degree of belief.

title: Mathematical Understanding
text: This website contains long-form articles investigating how mathematical meaning is constructed, preserved, and communicated. The articles examine mathematics not only as a formal system, but also as an activity performed through language, notation, diagrams, conceptual models, and human reasoning.

title: Mathematical Understanding
text:
1 Articles

1.1 Before Mathematics Begins
How Language and Visual Representation Shape Mathematical Understanding
An investigation into the representational work that occurs before formal mathematical reasoning can begin.


1.2 When Symbols Stay the Same but Meaning Changes
A Cognitive Framework for Preserving Context in Mathematical Reasoning
An examination of how learners silently change the conceptual interpretation of a mathematical expression while its visible symbols remain unchanged.

title: Before Mathematics Begins
text:
When students struggle with mathematics, the difficulty is usually attributed to weak computational skills, insufficient logical ability, or an incomplete understanding of the underlying concept. This diagnosis appears reasonable because mathematics is commonly viewed as a discipline of numbers, symbols, equations, and formal reasoning. If a learner produces an incorrect answer, it therefore seems natural to conclude that the mathematical reasoning itself has failed.
This view overlooks a process that begins before formal mathematical reasoning becomes possible. A learner cannot reason about an explanation merely because the explanation has been presented. Spoken words, written sentences, mathematical symbols, diagrams, gestures, and visual cues constitute external representations of mathematical ideas. These representations must first be perceived and interpreted. The learner then constructs an internal representation—a mental structure that captures the meaning and relationships conveyed by the external representations. It is this internal representation, rather than the external representations themselves, upon which mathematical reasoning operates.
The following diagram presents a deliberately simplified first model of this process. Its purpose is not to claim that understanding always develops through a rigid sequence, but to make visible the representational work that precedes explicit mathematical reasoning.






flowchart LR
    A["External representations"] --> B["Perception and interpretation"]
    B --> C["Internal representations"]
    C --> D["Organized mental model"]
    D --> E["Mathematical reasoning"]




Figure 1: A simplified first model of the representational work that makes mathematical reasoning possible.





An internal representation is not yet necessarily a coherent mental model. A learner may remember the appearance of a symbol, understand the meaning of an individual sentence, or visualize part of a diagram without understanding how these elements are related. A mental model arises when such internal representations become organized into a structure that supports explanation, inference, prediction, or further reasoning. The movement from external presentation to understanding is therefore not simply the transfer of information into the mind; it is the construction of relationships among internally represented elements.
This process is also not entirely linear. An emerging mental model influences what the learner notices when returning to the explanation. Initial reasoning may reveal a contradiction or missing relationship, causing the learner to reinterpret an earlier sentence, symbol, or diagram. Understanding therefore develops through repeated movement between external representations, interpretation, mental-model construction, and reasoning.






flowchart LR
    A["External representations"] --> B["Interpretation"]
    B --> C["Emerging mental model"]
    C --> D["Mathematical reasoning"]
    C -. "guides attention" .-> A
    D -. "reveals gaps" .-> B




Figure 2: Understanding develops recursively: emerging models guide attention, while reasoning reveals where reinterpretation is needed.





A useful, though incomplete, analogy is that of constructing a building. Delivering bricks, beams, windows, and doors to a construction site does not produce a usable house. The materials must be organized according to a structure. Mathematical explanations create a more demanding problem, however, because their components do not always arrive with their roles already established. A learner may have to determine what a symbol means, identify which sentence explains it, discover how a diagram corresponds to it, and infer the larger structure connecting them. The learner is therefore not merely assembling known materials according to an available blueprint; the learner may be identifying the materials, reconstructing the blueprint, and building the structure at the same time.






flowchart TD
    A["Representational components"] --> B["What does each component mean?"]
    A --> C["How are the components related?"]
    B --> D["Emerging structure"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Usable mental model"]




Figure 3: The learner must often infer both the meanings of representational components and the structure that connects them.





This hidden work is easy to overlook because expertise changes how representations are perceived. An experienced learner does not merely interpret the same isolated pieces more quickly. Familiar elements have become organized into larger, meaningful units. For example, a beginner may initially encounter \(y=2x\) as several symbols whose individual roles must be decoded. A more experienced learner may recognize the expression as a single relational structure: one quantity varies at twice the rate of another, and the same relationship can be represented by a table, a graph, or a verbal description. The difference between beginners and experts therefore cannot be reduced to general intelligence or processing speed. An explanation that ignores this difference may be mathematically correct while still requiring the beginner to reconstruct connections that the expert no longer notices as separate.






flowchart TD
    A["The expression y = 2x"] --> B["Beginning learner"]
    A --> C["Experienced learner"]
    B --> D["y, equals sign, 2, and x treated as separate elements"]
    C --> E["One relationship connecting two varying quantities"]
    D --> F["Additional work needed to coordinate the elements"]
    E --> G["Immediate access to related meanings and representations"]




Figure 4: Experience changes the organization of what is perceived: isolated elements become a coordinated relational structure.





This does not mean that every mathematical difficulty is merely a linguistic or visual misunderstanding. Learners may also struggle because of missing prerequisite knowledge, inaccurate concepts, insufficient fluency, anxiety, limitations of attention, or the inherent complexity of the mathematical relationships involved. The more defensible claim is that many difficulties that become visible during mathematical reasoning originate, at least partly, in the representational work required before and during that reasoning. Because mathematical thought operates through representations, representational difficulty and mathematical difficulty cannot always be separated cleanly.
The purpose of explanatory design is not to eliminate all cognitive effort. Understanding necessarily requires work: learners must compare cases, identify relationships, coordinate representations, test interpretations, and revise incomplete models. The relevant distinction is between productive work that contributes directly to understanding and avoidable work created by the explanation itself. Ambiguous language, unexplained notation, inconsistent terminology, poorly aligned diagrams, and disordered presentation can force learners to spend cognitive resources repairing the explanation before they can investigate the mathematics.






flowchart LR
    A["Learner's cognitive effort"] --> B["Productive construction"]
    A --> C["Avoidable interpretive repair"]
    B --> D["Compare, connect, infer, and revise"]
    C --> E["Decode ambiguity, resolve inconsistency, and reconstruct order"]
    D --> F["Stronger mental model"]
    E --> G["Resources diverted before mathematical reasoning"]




Figure 5: Explanatory design should preserve productive intellectual work while reducing avoidable interpretive repair.





Good explanatory design does not remove the work of understanding. It protects that work from being displaced by unnecessary interpretation, reorganization, and ambiguity resolution. Mathematical correctness is therefore necessary but insufficient. An explanation must also be designed so that its language, notation, visual structure, and sequence help the intended relationships become perceptible and support the construction of a usable mental model.
The purpose of this article is to investigate this usually hidden stage of mathematical understanding. Rather than asking only how mathematical ideas should be stated, demonstrated, or computed, it asks a more fundamental question:

How should mathematical explanations be designed so that they align with the processes through which the human mind constructs meaning?

The sections that follow will examine how language, mathematical notation, diagrams, spatial organization, sequencing, and prior knowledge interact during this construction. The subject of the investigation is not any one branch of mathematics, but the design of explanations that make mathematical relationships available for thought.

title: Before Mathematics Begins
section: 1.1 The Hidden Work of Reconstructing Relationships
text:
1.1 The Hidden Work of Reconstructing Relationships
When an explanation leaves important relationships implicit, the learner must supply the missing organization. The learner must determine the role of each piece of information, identify which elements refer to the same object or quantity, infer how those elements are connected, arrange them into a coherent structure, and test whether the resulting interpretation is consistent with the rest of the explanation. These activities may occur rapidly and interactively rather than as a fixed sequence, but separating them analytically allows us to see the cognitive work that a poorly organized explanation transfers to the learner.
The first requirement is role assignment. In the tank example, the values \(120\), \(15\), and \(t\) are not meaningful merely because they have been identified. The learner must determine that \(120\) is an initial quantity, \(15\) is a rate, and \(t\) is a duration. A number does not announce its mathematical role by itself. The role emerges from the language surrounding it, the units attached to it, and its relationship to other quantities. If these cues are weak, the learner must search the explanation and infer the intended function of each value.
The second requirement is reference binding: the learner must determine which words, symbols, and visual elements refer to the same underlying quantity. The phrases “water remaining,” “quantity in the tank,” and \(Q(t)\), for example, may all refer to one changing quantity. Unless this identity is made clear, the learner may preserve them as separate pieces of information rather than treating them as different representations of the same object. Similar difficulty arises when a diagram uses one label, the prose uses another, and an equation introduces a third without explicitly connecting them.
The third requirement is relational integration. After assigning roles and binding references, the learner must determine how the quantities affect one another. Knowing that \(15\) is a rate and \(t\) is a duration is not sufficient. The learner must infer that multiplying them produces the total amount removed after \(t\) minutes. The learner must then connect that accumulated loss to the initial quantity through subtraction. The mathematical model emerges only when these local relationships are integrated into a larger structure.






flowchart TD
    A["Presented information<br/>120, 15, t, Q(t)"] --> B["Assign roles<br/>initial value, rate, duration, result"]
    B --> C["Bind references<br/>connect words, symbols, and quantities"]
    C --> D["Infer local relationships<br/>rate × time = accumulated change"]
    D --> E["Construct a global structure<br/>initial quantity − accumulated change"]
    E --> F["Test for coherence<br/>Does the model fit the situation?"]
    F -. "revise if necessary" .-> B




Figure 7: When relationships remain implicit, the learner must reconstruct the organization required for reasoning.





The final requirement is coherence testing. A learner must decide whether the constructed model behaves as the described situation should behave. If no time has passed, substituting \(t=0\) should produce the initial quantity:
\[
Q(0)=120-15(0)=120.
\]
After one minute, the quantity should be \(15\) litres smaller:
\[
Q(1)=120-15(1)=105.
\]
These substitutions do more than calculate answers. They test whether the inferred structure preserves the meaning of the original situation. If the learner had written \(Q(t)=15-120t\), the expression would contain the same visible numbers and variable, yet the checks would reveal that their roles and relationships had been organized incorrectly. The reconstruction process therefore involves several forms of uncertainty. The learner may be uncertain about what an element represents, which elements belong together, which operation expresses their relationship, or whether the completed structure is consistent with the situation.

title: Before Mathematics Begins
section: 1.2 Productive Inference and Avoidable Interpretive Repair
text:
1.2 Productive Inference and Avoidable Interpretive Repair
The previous discussion creates a possible misunderstanding. If implicit relationships require additional cognitive work, it may appear that every effective explanation should state every relationship explicitly. Such a conclusion would be mistaken. Mathematical learning depends upon inference. Learners must discover patterns, coordinate ideas, test conjectures, and construct relationships that have not been stated in advance. An explanation that removes all such work may become easy to follow while providing little opportunity for understanding to develop. The purpose of explanatory design is therefore not to eliminate difficulty, but to distinguish difficulty that serves the learning objective from difficulty produced accidentally by the presentation.
We can call the first kind of work productive inference. Productive inference occurs when the learner’s effort is directed toward the mathematical relationship that the lesson intends to develop. If learners are studying how verbal descriptions can be converted into linear models, asking them to determine that the tank is represented by \(Q(t)=120-15t\) may be entirely appropriate. Identifying the initial value, interpreting the rate, and deciding how elapsed time affects the remaining quantity are then not obstacles surrounding the lesson; they are the substance of the lesson.
The second kind of work can be called avoidable interpretive repair. It occurs when the learner must resolve a problem created by the explanation rather than investigate the intended mathematical idea. Suppose a textbook describes the remaining water as \(Q(t)\), labels the same quantity as \(W\) in a diagram, and then uses \(A(t)\) in an equation without explaining that all three symbols refer to the same quantity. Determining whether \(Q(t)\), \(W\), and \(A(t)\) are equivalent does not deepen the learner’s understanding of linear change. It consumes effort because the external representations have not been coordinated.









Kind of cognitive work
Source of the difficulty
What the learner must do
Relationship to the learning objective




Productive inference
The mathematical relationship being studied
Compare, infer, connect, test, or generalize
Directly develops the intended understanding


Interpretive repair
Ambiguity, inconsistency, omission, or poor coordination in the explanation
Decode terminology, reconcile labels, recover references, or reconstruct presentation order
Consumes effort without directly developing the intended understanding









flowchart TD
    A["Cognitive effort required"] --> B{"What produces the effort?"}
    B -->|"The target relationship"| C["Productive inference"]
    B -->|"A defect in presentation"| D["Interpretive repair"]
    C --> E["Constructs intended understanding"]
    D --> F["Diverts resources from intended understanding"]




Figure 8: The educational value of cognitive effort depends on whether it is directed toward the intended object of learning.





Determining whether an inference is productive requires attention to prior knowledge. A relationship that is a reasonable object of discovery for one learner may be an inaccessible prerequisite for another. Similarly, a connection that an expert regards as obvious may require substantial reconstruction from a beginner. Explanatory design must therefore consider not only the mathematical content and instructional objective, but also the knowledge that the intended reader can reasonably be expected to bring to the explanation.
The central design principle can now be stated more precisely:

Make prerequisite relationships visible; preserve target relationships for meaningful inference; remove accidental obstacles created by the presentation.
